one_line_takeaway: Using a commodity LLM as an automated black-box prompt optimizer plus a stacked injection suffix, AutoJailbreak reliably coaxes GPT-4V into naming real people from photos (best prompt 95.3% ASR, ~0.76 avg ASR on US celebrities), while incidentally exposing a strong Western-vs-Asian recognition bias.
key_finding: The single best generated prompt reaches ASR=95.3% on the American-celebrity subset (64 trials per image), and the weak-to-strong template consistently beats the traditional template: on GPT-4 average RSR is 20.01% (W2S) vs 14.98% (traditional), and the fraction of prompts with RSR>70% is 8.59% (W2S) vs 5.53% (traditional). Against baselines the optimized prompts dominate — on American celebrities average ASR 0.76 / RSR 0.75 vs the injection baseline's 0.04 / 0.04. A notable secondary finding is regional bias: GPT-4V recognizes American celebrities far more than Asian ones (American RSR ~53% higher than Chinese, ASR ~44% higher; American avg ASR 0.76 vs Chinese 0.32 vs Thai 0.21).
core_contribution: The paper introduces AutoJailbreak, an automated black-box method that uses an off-the-shelf LLM (GPT-3.5-turbo or GPT-4) as a red-team optimizer to generate natural-language jailbreak prompts that induce GPT-4V to identify real individuals (celebrities) in images, defeating GPT-4V's safety alignment that refuses facial identification. The method's three novel pieces are: (1) a 'weak-to-strong' in-context prompting template that supplies the red-team model with a short low-quality (weak) prompt and a high-quality (strong, RSR>70%) prompt as contrastive examples to elicit better jailbreak prompts; (2) 'suffix-based attack enhancement' that appends a combination injection (prefix injection + refusal suppression + length control) to each generated prompt; and (3) an early-stopping 'efficient search with hypothesis testing' that abandons a candidate prompt after n_ES=5 consecutive recognition failures, framed as a binomial hypothesis test with type-I error 0.005. Distinguish from cited work: PAIR (Chao et al.), AutoDAN (Liu et al.), FigStep (Gong et al.), visual-adversarial jailbreaks (Qi et al.), and the authors' own prior GPT-4V self-adversarial system-prompt attack (Wu et al. 2024, which supplies the celebrity list and metrics) are all Related Work / baselines, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only API access to GPT-4V (gpt-4-vision-preview) — no model weights, no gradients, no manual prompt engineering. The attacker supplies their own auxiliary LLM (GPT-3.5/GPT-4) as a red-team prompt generator. The adversarial goal is a privacy violation: forcing GPT-4V to name a real person (public-figure/celebrity) from a photo, which safety alignment (RLHF + moderation) is designed to refuse. Success is measured only by whether the model emits a human name (ASR) or the correct name (RSR); no white-box access or fine-tuning is assumed.
limitations: (1) The headline 95.3% ASR is the best single prompt, not the average — average American ASR is 0.76, Chinese 0.32, Thai 0.21, and most generated prompts fail entirely (e.g. 58.08% of GPT-4 W2S prompts and 95.45% of ChatGPT traditional prompts have RSR=0%). (2) Scope is narrow: only the facial-identification-refusal safety behavior, only celebrities GPT-4V already recognizes (dataset seeded from prior work), 17 celebrities x 10 images per region. (3) The adversarial-image baseline is not a fair comparison — for lack of open-source targets it attacks a VAE encoder rather than GPT-4V itself, so its poor scores (American avg RSR 0.2625) mainly show VAE-perturbations don't transfer, not that image attacks are inherently weak on GPT-4V. (4) Authors' own stated limitations: attack scope limited to identification (no address/CAPTCHA tasks, no MLLM-safety benchmark) and no cost-effective defense proposed (LLM input/output filtering is expensive). (5) Results are tied to a specific 2023-2024 gpt-4-vision-preview snapshot and may not reproduce on updated models.

datasets_used: Self-compiled celebrity face dataset of 3 regional groups (American, Chinese, Thai), 17 celebrities per group, 10 images per celebrity, seeded from celebrities GPT-4V is known to recognize (Wu et al. 2024)
American celebrity images sourced from Celebrity Face Image Dataset (THAKUR 2022) and CelebA (Liu et al. 2015)
Chinese celebrity images from 'Face datasets of ten chinese stars' (shujujishi 2019) plus top Google search results
Thai celebrity images curated from top Google search results
quantitative_results: Best single jailbreak prompt: ASR = 95.3% on American celebrities (64 trials per image)
GPT-4 red-team: average RSR 20.01% (weak-to-strong) vs 14.98% (traditional); RSR>0.7 prompts 8.59% vs 5.53%; RSR=0 prompts 58.08% vs 68.84%
ChatGPT red-team: average RSR 9.66% (W2S) vs 1.52% (traditional); RSR>0.7 3.54% vs 0.00%; RSR=0 77.78% vs 95.45%
American celebrities avg: ASR 0.76, RSR 0.75 (injection baseline injASR 0.04, injRSR 0.04)
Chinese celebrities avg: ASR 0.32, RSR 0.22 (injASR 0.02, injRSR 0.02)
Thai celebrities avg: ASR 0.21, RSR 0.09 (injASR 0.00, injRSR 0.00)
Regional bias: American RSR ~53% higher and ASR ~44% higher than Chinese celebrities
Weak-to-strong template saves at least 36.2% of input token quota vs traditional in-context learning
Zero-shot pool construction: 1200 candidate prompts -> 54 initially successful -> 3 prompts with RSR>70% as initial pool
Adversarial image (VisAttack) baseline avg RSR/ASR: American 0.2625/0.325 vs Ours 0.76/0.764; Chinese 0.0375/0.2 vs Ours 0.26/0.322; Thai 0.00625/0.1625 vs Ours 0.068/0.148
baselines_compared: Combination Injection Attack (prefix injection + refusal suppression + length control suffix, no optimized jailbreak prompt)
Adversarial Image Attack (VisAttack, Dong et al. 2023 style, optimized against a VAE encoder with perturbation budget delta=32/255)
Traditional (one-shot) in-context learning template, as the internal comparison against the proposed weak-to-strong template
